proof_stages.label: Qualitative alpha-expander framework
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes the external-neighborhood definition of an alpha-expander, treats alpha as a small parameter, and records basic consequences such as exponential ball growth, connectivity, logarithmic diameter, rapid mixing under bounded degree, and large separators.

proof_stages.label: Sources and obstruction benchmarks for alpha-expanders
proof_stages.summary: Concrete dense, bipartite, sparse, subdivided, and spectral-gap constructions show that the definition is nonvacuous while revealing parity, congruence, and embedding limitations on possible path and cycle theorems.

proof_stages.label: Path/cycle questions and DFS invariants
proof_stages.summary: The central questions about longest paths, longest cycles, and the set of cycle lengths are formulated, and DFS is developed with the key invariants that vertices move one at a time, no processed vertex sees an unvisited vertex, the active stack is a path, and every non-tree edge connects an ancestor to a descendant.

proof_stages.label: DFS long-path lemma
proof_stages.summary: Stopping DFS when the processed set has prescribed size converts a one-scale expansion lower bound into a path whose length is the neighborhood size, giving paths of length at least alpha n/2 in every alpha-expander.

proof_stages.label: DFS-tree long-cycle construction
proof_stages.summary: A DFS tree is split below a vertex into a large union of subtrees whose expansion must attach far up the root path, and the far attachment closes a cycle of length at least alpha n/4.

proof_stages.label: Spread and abundance of cycle lengths
proof_stages.summary: After comparing logarithmic short cycles with linear long cycles, the talk states an additive O(1/alpha) approximation theorem for all lengths in a large interval and then proves a polynomial-in-alpha lower bound for the number of distinct cycle lengths using a BFS tree, a long outside path, reachability into layers, and branching cycles.

proof_stages.label: Exact prescribed lengths under large-set adjacency
proof_stages.summary: Strengthening expansion to require an edge between every two disjoint beta n-sets, one first cleans the graph to a good induced expander and then embeds a double-ended broom tree whose two leaf sets are joined by the large-set property to obtain every cycle length in an almost-spanning interval.

proof_stages.label: Optimality and modular cycle-length questions
proof_stages.summary: The concluding discussion conjectures the optimal alpha-linear order for the number of distinct cycle lengths and asks for expansion-based guarantees of cycle lengths in specified congruence classes.

examples.name: Subdividing every edge of an expander
examples.note: Stretching each edge by a common factor gives sparse alpha-expanders whose cycle lengths all lie in one congruence class modulo the stretch length, explaining the unavoidable O(1/alpha) gaps and embedding obstructions.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker runs the edge-subdivision construction and reasons out its effects on expansion, cycle-length divisibility, and adjacent degree-three vertices

passes.reason: Uniformly subdividing all edges gives an extremal sparse cycle-length pattern, used as an obstruction to arbitrary cycle lengths and certain embeddings.
